items.title: BTC Rejected at $66K, Breaks to High $50s on Continuous Spot Selling
items.idea: BTC traded into the $66K range, was rejected, and broke down into the high $50s amid continuous spot selling. Smart money on-chain data (per Seven C) shows no significant accumulation at current levels. Stablecoin flows, token unlocks, and prediction market positioning are the secondary signals to watch for the next leg.
items.angle: The on-chain absence of smart money accumulation at these levels is bearish confirmation, not just price action. If stablecoins do not flow back into DeFi yield at these prices, this is not a dip — it is distribution.
items.tweetA: BTC rejected at $66K, continuous spot selling pushed it to the high $50s. The leverage flush is obvious. What is less obvious: smart money on-chain is not buying the dip. That divergence between price and positioning is your signal — not the candle.
items.tweetB: Everyone is watching the BTC chart. I am watching stablecoin flows. When stables start moving back into DeFi yield at these levels, that is the accumulation signal. Until then, this is a spot-seller market, not a dip-buyer market.

items.title: Cursor Acquired for $60B — Largest Startup Exit in History
items.idea: Cursor grew from an internal prototype to enterprise software and was acquired for $60B — the largest startup acquisition on record — with business customers now generating most of its revenue. The acquirer is shifting strategy toward owning complete AI software stacks rather than competing on standalone tools. For context, GitHub sold for $7.5B in 2018 and Copilot launched in 2021.
items.angle: The coding workflow just repriced by a full order of magnitude in 8 years. If Cursor is worth $60B, the second-place IDE market cap math is now live. Every developer tool company without a strategic acquirer is re-underwriting their exit.
items.tweetA: Cursor: internal prototype to $60B acquisition. That is not a coding tool exit — that is someone buying the entire AI software stack before they can no longer afford to. The race to own the IDE-to-deployment pipeline just got very expensive to enter late.
items.tweetB: GitHub: $7.5B in 2018. Cursor: $60B in 2026. The coding workflow repriced 8x in 8 years. And Cursor is not the only player. What does Windsurf, Zed, or the next IDE fetch if someone else still needs to own the stack? The M&A math across all developer tooling just changed.

items.title: Anthropic Alleges Coordinated Model Extraction via Millions of Fake Account Interactions
items.idea: Anthropic alleges that thousands of fake accounts ran millions of Claude interactions to systematically distill and copy model behavior. The company is pushing for stricter export controls and penalties specifically targeting coordinated model distillation — the first time a major lab has explicitly named this as an IP threat requiring legislative action.
items.angle: The uncomfortable question: if frontier models can be distilled through API calls, what is the sustainable moat? Not weights, not architecture. Maybe just the data flywheel and the regulatory permission slip. Whoever wins the political fight wins the market.
items.tweetA: Anthropic: thousands of fake accounts, millions of Claude interactions, systematic model distillation at industrial scale. Now they want export controls specifically targeting distillation. We are about to find out whether IP law can keep pace with gradient descent.
items.tweetB: The real question in the Anthropic extraction story: if you can distill a frontier model through API calls, what is the sustainable competitive moat? Not the weights. Not the architecture. Maybe just the data flywheel and the regulatory permission slip. The company that wins the political fight wins the market.

items.title: AI Chip Rally Adds $400B in Market Value on Micron and Qualcomm Strong Outlooks
items.idea: Strong outlooks from Micron and Qualcomm lifted the semiconductor complex by $400B after a sharp pullback. This is occurring simultaneously with Ironsides Macro flagging AI capex financing saturation and Dark Side of the Boom reporting SpaceX bond wobble — conflicting equity vs credit signals that rarely stay wrong simultaneously for long.
items.angle: Either the equity market is right that the chip cycle continues, or the credit signal in AI-adjacent bonds is right. History says when these two diverge, watch the credit.
items.tweetA: AI chip complex +$400B on Micron and Qualcomm outlooks. Meanwhile, Ironsides is calling AI capex financing saturation and Dark Side of the Boom is seeing SpaceX bond wobble. Either equity is ignoring the credit signal, or the credit signal is noise. History says watch the credit. $NVDA $MU $QCOM
items.tweetB: Every AI chip rally is now a function of whether hyperscalers keep spending. And the hyperscalers are borrowing to fund capex. We are one credit event away from finding out what AI hardware demand looks like without sovereign-backed balance sheets behind it.

items.title: Anthropic Models Pulled Offline for Export Order — Legal Challenge Filed Immediately
items.idea: New national security restrictions forced Anthropic to take two flagship models offline. A legal startup filed suit arguing the restrictions immediately disrupted its business and international workforce. Cybersecurity community argues removal weakens security more than it protects — the first institutional pushback on AI export controls as a framework.
items.angle: The precedent set in this lawsuit matters enormously. If export controls on model weights survive legal challenge, every model lab operates under a new risk: capability removal with 24 hours notice.
items.tweetA: Two Anthropic models pulled offline by government export order. Legal startup files suit within days arguing immediate business disruption. Cybersecurity community says the removal weakens security more than it protects it. First real test of AI export controls in court. Precedent set here rewrites regulatory risk for every model lab.
items.tweetB: Anthropic offline by order. The second-order consequence nobody modeled: companies built workflows on these models and now face international workforce disruption. This is what regulatory risk looks like in the AI era — not a fine, but a capability removal with 24-hour notice.

items.title: GitHub Posts Record Month After Switching Copilot to Usage-Based Pricing on June 1
items.idea: GitHub switched Copilot to usage-based pricing June 1 and posted its strongest month on record. Usage-based pricing in AI-native developer tools appears to increase engagement rather than reduce it — inverting the conventional SaaS playbook where metered pricing suppresses usage.
items.angle: If developers expand usage when charged per interaction rather than capping it, the unit economics for AI tooling are better than the market assumed. $MSFT may have found the optimal pricing model for AI-native products.
items.tweetA: GitHub switches Copilot to usage-based pricing June 1. Posts record month. When you charge developers per AI interaction instead of per seat, they use MORE, not less. This is the opposite of every SaaS assumption. Usage-based pricing for AI-native tools is not a revenue risk — it is a demand signal.
items.tweetB: GitHub record month on usage-based Copilot is the live experiment every enterprise AI vendor is watching. Developers do not cap usage when charged per token, they expand it. The flywheel is real. $MSFT

items.title: Cerebras Revenue Nearly Doubles YoY — Stock Falls on Weak Margin Guidance
items.idea: Cerebras posted near-doubling revenue YoY but investors focused on weaker margin guidance from 'temporary infrastructure arrangements.' The market is separating AI infrastructure revenue from profitability — a distinction that did not matter in 2024 but clearly matters now.
items.angle: The difference between temporary and structural margin drag is the difference between a buying opportunity and a short. These questions are not being asked loudly enough.
items.tweetA: Cerebras revenue nearly doubled YoY. Stock fell because margins are weak. At what point does the AI infrastructure market stop rewarding growth and start punishing the cost of that growth? That inflection may be closer than the bulls think.
items.tweetB: Buried in the Cerebras note: 'temporary infrastructure arrangements weighing on profitability.' Which arrangements? What margin drag? For how long? The difference between temporary and structural is the difference between a trade and a short. $CBRS

items.title: Deep Expertise Now More Valuable Than General Coding as AI Automates Implementation Work
items.idea: AI has reduced the value of routine implementation work that commanded premium engineering salaries. Engineers focused on reliability, scale, and complex production systems are gaining pricing power. The labor market for technical talent is bifurcating: those who understand why systems fail at 10M requests vs those who can write code in greenfield conditions.
items.angle: Companies with teams in the high-value tier gain durable cost advantages. The human capital gap between these two categories is growing faster than the equity market is pricing.
items.tweetA: AI automated the 'just implement it' work. Engineers gaining pricing power now are those who understand why something fails at 10M requests, not just how to write it. This was always the right skill hierarchy. AI just accelerated the market's discovery of who actually has it.
items.tweetB: Engineering labor market is splitting: routine implementation automated, reliability and scale expertise getting more expensive. In 5 years the bottleneck will not be 'can someone write the code' — it will be 'does someone understand the system well enough not to destroy it under load.'

items.title: Seedcamp Fund III: 20x Net Return, 50.3% Net IRR — Closes $320M Fund VII
items.idea: Seedcamp Fund III tracking 20x net return and 50.3% net IRR from early investments in Revolut, UiPath, and Pleo. Just closed $320M for Fund VII across First Check and Select follow-on vehicles. The Select strategy suggests disciplined concentration in winners rather than equal-weighted seed bets.
items.angle: 50.3% net IRR out of European seed VC challenges the narrative that US dominates early-stage returns. The LP conversation around European seed is no longer a second-tier pitch.
items.tweetA: Seedcamp Fund III: 20x net return, 50.3% net IRR. Backed Revolut, UiPath, Pleo from seed. Just closed $320M Fund VII. European early-stage VC is having its moment. The narrative that the US wins early-stage and Europe gets left behind is getting harder to sustain.
items.tweetB: 50.3% net IRR from European seed VC. Most US seed funds would take that. The Select follow-on strategy — concentrating into winners — is the operational alpha on top of picking. $320M Fund VII is now deploying.

items.title: Harvey Token Usage 12x in 5 Months — Enterprise Shifting from Consumption to ROI Metrics
items.idea: Harvey's token consumption climbed 12x in five months, but companies are separating expensive AI workloads from routine tasks, with focus shifting from measuring consumption to proving business value and financial return. Legal will be the first enterprise sector where AI ROI is measurable on a per-matter basis.
items.angle: 12x token growth only sustains if the ROI is demonstrable. Legal provides the perfect stress test with billable hour comparisons as the explicit benchmark.
items.tweetA: Harvey token usage 12x in 5 months. Enterprises are separating expensive workloads from routine tasks. The next wave of AI cost management is not switching models — it is workload routing. The firms that figure this out will have structural cost advantages.
items.tweetB: Token usage 12x in 5 months at Harvey. Real question: did case outcomes improve 12x? Legal will be the first enterprise sector where AI ROI is measurable per matter, with billable hour rates as the benchmark. If Harvey shows it, it changes the conversation for every enterprise AI deployment.

items.title: Bezos: AI Will Reduce Permitting Delays, Raise Productivity, Shift More Families to Single Incomes
items.idea: Jeff Bezos argues AI will raise productivity, reduce government permitting delays, and make government more efficient. He predicts higher AI-driven productivity could shift more families toward single incomes — implications for housing, consumer spending, and labor force participation that current macro models do not capture.
items.angle: The permitting thesis is the most underpriced: if AI accelerates approval processes for housing, infrastructure, and energy, it unlocks physical supply that monetary policy cannot touch. Structurally disinflationary.
items.tweetA: Bezos on AI: it will reduce government permitting delays. The most underpriced macro thesis. If AI speeds up housing, infrastructure, and energy permitting — it unlocks supply in the physical world that monetary policy cannot touch. That is structurally disinflationary.
items.tweetB: Bezos predicts AI productivity will shift more families toward single incomes. If right: fewer cars, different housing demand, different consumer spending. The labor market models do not model this. Second-order effects of AI on household structure may be bigger than first-order productivity effects.

items.title: OpenAI Jalapeno ASIC Built by Broadcom in 9 Months — ASIC Design Timeline Collapsing
items.idea: OpenAI's Jalapeno inference ASIC was designed by Broadcom from start to finish in 9 months, with the design 'accelerated by OpenAI models.' Standard ASIC timeline: 2-3 years. If EDA software combined with AI-assisted design flows is structurally compressing silicon development cycles, the cost curve for custom AI inference chips is about to inflect downward faster than the market models.
items.angle: If 9-month ASIC cycles become standard for digital inference chips, every hyperscaler can build their own inference chip in under a year. $NVDA custom silicon competition accelerates. $AVGO's ASIC design services business model faces structural disruption.
items.tweetA: Jalapeno: OpenAI inference ASIC, designed by Broadcom start to finish in 9 months, using OpenAI models in the design flow. Standard ASIC: 2-3 years. If AI is now compressing silicon design cycles 3-4x, the moat in custom AI inference chips just got dramatically shallower. $AVGO $NVDA
items.tweetB: Jalapeno in 9 months either means Broadcom is genuinely elite or AI-assisted EDA is collapsing the timeline. If the latter: every hyperscaler can now credibly build their own inference chip in under a year. The market is not pricing this as a commoditization risk.

items.title: Claude Code Burns 578K Input Tokens vs Codex on Same Task — Context Accumulation Is the Cost Driver
items.idea: Raschka benchmarked Claude Code vs Codex: Claude Code used ~578K input tokens but only 4.5K output tokens across 25 turns on the same coding task where Codex used far fewer. The difference is not output quality — Claude accumulates full conversation history, tool calls, and file contents across each turn. Harness choice matters as much as model choice for token cost at scale.
items.angle: For enterprises deploying AI coding agents at production scale, the harness is a bigger cost center than model pricing. If Claude Code's context accumulation is structural, enterprises need to model harness costs explicitly.
items.tweetA: Raschka measured it: Claude Code uses 578K input tokens vs 4.5K output on a 25-turn coding task. Codex uses a fraction of that. Not quality — Claude accumulates the full context history each turn. Running Claude Code at enterprise scale means the harness is a bigger cost center than the model.
items.tweetB: Claude Code vs Codex token comparison: Claude accumulates full conversation history each turn, Codex does not. Same task, roughly 10x the input token cost. Speed at equal quality favors Codex. This is the pricing math enterprises will run in 2026 when AI coding agents become a budget line item.

items.title: Qwen3.6 35B-A3B: 40 tok/sec on Mac Mini, Best Local Coding Agent in Its Size Class
items.idea: Qwen3.6 35B-A3B runs at 40 tok/sec on a Mac Mini M4, 22GB download, 30-40GB RAM. Dominates sub-40B benchmark class. Scores 4/5 on Raschka's coding agent evaluation vs 1/5 for North Mini Code 1.0 and 0/5 for Gemma 4 E2B. At 40 tok/sec it matches GPT 5.5 Pro subscription speed. The quality gap between local and cloud is closing.
items.angle: The local inference inflection point for coding agents has arrived at consumer hardware prices. The total cost of ownership math is getting interesting for enterprise deployments where privacy or fixed costs matter.
items.tweetA: Qwen3.6 35B-A3B at 40 tok/sec on a Mac Mini M4 — same speed as GPT 5.5 through a Pro subscription. For coding tasks where privacy or fixed cost budgets matter, the quality gap between local open-weight and cloud frontier is closing faster than the industry admits.
items.tweetB: Qwen3.6 35B-A3B: 22GB download, 30-40GB RAM, 40 tok/sec on Mac Mini. Scores 4/5 on coding agent eval vs 1/5 for North Mini Code. Dominates sub-40B benchmark class. The local inference inflection point for coding agents has arrived. You just need the hardware.

items.title: Qwen Code Sends Telemetry to Alibaba/Aliyun Endpoints by Default — Even With Local Ollama Models
items.idea: Even with a fully local Ollama model, Qwen Code sends session IDs, tool metadata, and model info to Alibaba/Aliyun endpoints by default. Fix requires explicit settings in ~/.qwen/settings.json. Claude Code reportedly sends data to both Anthropic and Datadog. The fully local coding agent setup is not actually local without deliberate configuration.
items.angle: For enterprises evaluating local AI coding stacks on sovereignty or compliance grounds, default telemetry behavior creates exposure most evaluators miss. The harness layer is a hidden data egress risk underneath every model discussion.
items.tweetA: Running Qwen Code with a local Ollama model and think you went fully local? By default, session IDs, tool metadata, and model info still go to Alibaba/Aliyun endpoints. The fix is documented. The default is exfiltration. Local AI stacks require reading the settings docs. Nobody does.
items.tweetB: Qwen Code telemetry to Aliyun, Codex to OpenAI, Claude Code to Anthropic plus Datadog. The supply chain risk in AI coding agents is not the model weights — it is what the harness does with your session metadata by default. Enterprise compliance teams are not asking the right questions yet.

items.title: Jalapeno's 9-Month Build by Broadcom Signals ASIC Design Moat May Be Gone
items.idea: Vik's framing: two conclusions from Jalapeno — Broadcom is elite, or ASIC design has become commodity. Analog, photonics, and non-digital chip design remain genuinely hard years-long work. The moat is migrating from digital logic to physical silicon. Digital inference ASICs: commoditizing. Optics, power delivery, analog: still a decade of specialization.
items.angle: If digital ASIC design is commoditizing, the investment thesis shifts from ASIC designers to optics and power delivery. Companies doing photonic interconnects and power delivery maintain defensibility that digital ASIC firms are losing.
items.tweetA: Vik on Jalapeno: ASIC moat may be gone for digital inference chips. But analog, optics, and power delivery are still years-long specialized work. The investment moat is migrating from digital to physical silicon. Know which you own.
items.tweetB: OpenAI press release says Jalapeno design was 'accelerated by OpenAI models.' If AI is now designing the chips that run AI, the feedback loop is real: better models make better chips make better models faster and cheaper. The self-improvement loop is not just software.

items.title: Qualcomm Acquires Modular: Mojo Language as the Android of AI Inference
items.idea: Qualcomm is acquiring Modular to become the software layer for disaggregated AI inference — mixing hardware (Qualcomm DragonFly, NVIDIA LPUs, AMD CPUs) under one Mojo/MAX software stack. Vik: if NVIDIA CUDA is iOS (proprietary lock-in), Mojo is Android (cross-hardware open standard). Critical uncertainty: does a Qualcomm-owned platform have credibility as hardware-agnostic?
items.angle: The Android-of-inference bet only works if hyperscalers adopt Mojo/MAX. They only adopt it if Qualcomm hardware is competitive enough to justify switching cost from CUDA. The software thesis depends entirely on the hardware thesis.
items.tweetA: Qualcomm acquires Modular. Thesis: Mojo as Android of AI inference — cross-hardware software for any silicon. If CUDA is iOS, Mojo is Android. But owned by Qualcomm. Would you build your AI stack on an Android owned by Samsung? That adoption question determines everything. $QCOM $NVDA
items.tweetB: Qualcomm buying Modular for disaggregated inference: DragonFly rack plus NVIDIA LPU plus AMD CPU under one Mojo software layer. Practice risk: does Mojo under Qualcomm ownership have credibility as a neutral CUDA alternative? That answer determines whether this is a real NVIDIA threat or just a press release.

items.title: Trump Admin Gates Frontier AI Models — Governance Shifts Economic Value from Training to Inference
items.idea: ChatGPT 5.6 'Sol' released with capabilities gated by Trump administration on a case-by-case basis. Vik's thesis: restricted user base plus finite compute creates neutral equilibrium for frontier models. Winner: inference infrastructure. Policy also creates structural disincentive to train next frontier model if deployment rights can be pre-restricted, shifting labs toward inference monetization.
items.angle: This is where AI governance becomes AI economics. If labs know their next frontier model might be restricted before deployment, training calculus changes. Inference becomes the long-term economic battlefield.
items.tweetA: Trump admin gates ChatGPT 5.6 Sol on a case-by-case basis. Vik's thesis: restricted frontier access plus finite compute puts inference infrastructure in a neutral equilibrium. If you cannot scale frontier access, every token of compute becomes more valuable. Inference accelerators benefit structurally.
items.tweetB: Government control of frontier AI release is now an established precedent. Not a one-off. The question is how labs price this into training decisions. If the next model will be restricted anyway, do you train it differently? The regulatory option embedded in frontier model training is not yet priced.

items.title: SambaNova Runs MiniMax M2.7 at 435 Tokens/sec — Frontier Speed Without the GPU Rack
items.idea: SambaNova's SambaCloud runs MiniMax M2.7 at 435 tok/sec — the fastest provider for this model. M2.7 ranks alongside Claude Opus 4.8 and GPT 5.4 on agentic tasks at a fraction of the cost. Typical frontier API: 40-60 tok/sec. At 435 tok/sec, long-horizon agent workflows are no longer speed-limited by generation — constraint shifts from latency to orchestration.
items.angle: At 435 tok/sec on a frontier-class model, you can run 5-10 parallel agent branches for the latency cost of a single chain. Not a marginal speed improvement — a different workflow regime.
items.tweetA: SambaNova running MiniMax M2.7 at 435 tok/sec. Typical frontier API: 40-60 tok/sec. M2.7 ranks alongside Claude Opus 4.8 on agentic tasks. At 10x the speed, long-horizon agent workflows stop being speed-limited. The architecture of production AI agents changes completely at this throughput.
items.tweetB: 435 tok/sec on a frontier-class model (MiniMax M2.7 via SambaCloud). At that throughput you can run 10 parallel agent branches for the latency cost of 1. Not a marginal improvement — a different regime. The teams figuring out how to use this are building products the rest cannot replicate yet.

items.title: Fed Reserve Management Purchases Likely Fueled AI/Semi Speculation — Now Ending
items.idea: Ironsides argues Fed Reserve Management Purchases (RMPs) contributed to speculative activity in equities, especially semiconductors and leveraged single-stock products. With the Fed showing signs of ending RMPs, aggregate liquidity may contract even as bank credit redirects toward productive investment. The AI/semi equity rally may have had an invisible Fed backstop now being removed without being called a tightening.
items.angle: A second-derivative liquidity risk that does not show up in the Fed funds rate. If RMPs were the invisible hand behind the semi rally, removing them is a tightening in effect without the label.
items.tweetA: Ironsides today: Fed Reserve Management Purchases (RMPs) likely fueled the semi and AI equity speculation. RMPs now ending. If true, the Fed is quietly removing the liquidity support structure for AI and semi equities without raising rates or calling it a tightening. Nobody is pricing this. $NVDA $SOXX
items.tweetB: The RMP-to-semis transmission: Fed injects liquidity via RMPs, excess reserves find high-beta AI and semi bets, speculation looks like organic demand. If this unwinds, the semiconductor rotation arrives without a fundamental explanation and analysts reach for earnings misses that have not happened yet.

charts.desc: Sector allocation table referenced in email — worth opening for changes

items.title: 1Q26 GDP Revision: Soft Consumption, Surging IP Investment — AI Capex Economy Is Bifurcating
items.idea: 1Q26 GDP revisions: softer consumption and private domestic demand but even stronger nonresidential fixed investment, particularly IP investment. AI-related capex is becoming more vulnerable to cyclical cooling. The US economy is bifurcating into an AI capex complex and everything else.
items.angle: The GDP composition is telling you the economy is being funded by AI capex, not consumer spending. These two legs cannot diverge indefinitely. When equity sentiment turns, the GDP composition flips quickly.
items.tweetA: 1Q26 GDP revision: soft consumption, private domestic demand weak. IP investment surging. The US economy is bifurcating — the AI capex complex and everyone else. Strategists pricing broad reflation are trading the wrong data series.
items.tweetB: Surging IP investment in 1Q26 GDP while consumption cools: companies are building AI infrastructure not generating consumer income yet. This only lasts as long as equity markets fund the capex cycle. When sentiment turns, the GDP composition flips fast and the narrative reverses hard.

items.title: Ironsides: AI Buildout Is Disinflationary — Pushes Back Hard on FOMC Hawks
items.idea: Ironsides directly rejects FOMC members arguing AI buildout is inflationary. Their argument: higher costs in one sector only become inflationary if fiscal and monetary policy accommodate. Weakening AI capex stock prices, rising capital intensity, and financing saturation suggest the buildout may instead pressure margins, compress valuations, and be disinflationary. Market confirmation: breakeven inflation declining sharply, bull steepening, trimmed-mean PCE near post-pandemic lows, CPI/PCE divergence at historically anomalous levels.
items.angle: When bond market and credit market are both pricing disinflation while the Fed talks inflation, one of them is wrong. The historical base rate on bond market vs FOMC hawks is not kind to the hawks.
items.tweetA: Ironsides contra the FOMC hawks: AI buildout is DISINFLATIONARY. Higher costs in one sector only inflate the broader economy if policy accommodates. If capex squeezes margins and compresses valuations instead, it is deflationary. Breakeven inflation falling. Bull steepening. Trimmed PCE near lows. The market agrees.
items.tweetB: Breakeven inflation declining sharply. Bull steepening in Treasuries. Trimmed-mean PCE near post-pandemic lows. CPI/PCE divergence historically anomalous. Bond market pricing disinflation. FOMC talking AI-driven inflation. One of them is wrong. History says watch the bond market.

charts.desc: Bull steepening and breakeven inflation chart worth saving — disinflation signal visible in the curve

items.title: Hudson River Trading $8B Revenue in 2024 — Doubled YoY, Beat Citadel Securities in Q2 2025
items.idea: HRT generated ~$8B in net trading revenue in 2024, nearly double 2023. Q2 2025: $2.62B vs Citadel Securities $2.39B. At $8B annual revenue, HRT is larger than most investment banks' entire trading P&L. These are utility-scale revenues from electronic market making — the talent arms race this funds flows back into asset markets.
items.angle: At this revenue scale, HFT firms are the liquidity infrastructure for everyone else. The talent competition creates a feedback loop: best math talent goes to market making, depleting the fundamental research bench.
items.tweetA: Hudson River Trading $8B net trading revenue in 2024 — nearly doubled from 2023. Beat Citadel Securities in Q2 2025 ($2.62B vs $2.39B). HRT is now larger than most bank trading P&Ls. At this scale HFT firms are not competing with hedge funds — they are the liquidity infrastructure the rest of the market depends on.
items.tweetB: HRT $8B, Citadel Securities trailing in Q2 2025. These revenues fund the most aggressive talent war in finance: $300K flat base for 22-year-old quants, $1.4M average comp at Jane Street. The market for quantitative talent is accelerating because the returns on deploying it are accelerating. AI has not changed this yet.

items.title: Jane Street $1.4M Average Comp Per Head in 2024 — 22-Year-Old Quants Clear $400K Year One
items.idea: Jane Street averaged $1.4M in compensation per head across ~3,000 staff in 2024. New grad quant traders earn $300K-$700K total comp. Citadel Securities acceptance rate: 1-2%, lower than any Ivy League school. A 22-year-old with the right math reflexes clears $400K year one. Flat $300K base across Jane Street and Five Rings (identical H-1B filings) signals competition is on the bonus, not the base.
items.angle: If AI were displacing quantitative talent, these numbers would be contracting. They are expanding. The quant comp data is a real-time signal that quantitative finance returns are still accelerating despite — or possibly because of — the AI buildout.
items.tweetA: Jane Street: $1.4M average comp across 3,000 staff in 2024. New grads: $300K-$700K. Citadel quant acceptance rate: 1-2%. 22-year-olds clear $400K year one. If AI were eating quantitative finance returns, these numbers would be going down. They are going up.
items.tweetB: Flat $300K base at Jane Street and Five Rings per identical H-1B filings. Competition is on the bonus — which means competition is on trading P&L per person. At $1.4M average, the marginal quant generates multiples of comp in edge. Miami leads base salaries at $242K because Citadel moved. Geography follows the firm.

items.title: SpaceX Bond Wobble Signals AI Funding Trade Starting to 'Charge Admission'
items.idea: Dark Side of the Boom argues that SpaceX bond deal showed classic momentum-demand confusion: the book looked enormous because everyone wanted an allocation, but then bonds broke and fast money looked for the next buyer. The AI funding trade has moved from 'is the dream real' to 'who is left holding the paper.' When even the most marquee AI-adjacent credit deal shows demand-momentum confusion on first-day trading, the marginal buyer of AI risk is shifting.
items.angle: Bond market leads equity. SpaceX bond wobble — the most desired AI-adjacent credit — is a leading indicator for the broader AI equity risk premium. Credit spreads on AI-adjacent names are worth watching now before the equity market catches up.
items.tweetA: Dark Side of the Boom: SpaceX bonds broke on first-day trading and fast money looked for the next buyer. The AI funding trade is starting to charge admission. The distinction between demand and momentum is becoming painfully obvious in AI credit. Bond market is saying something equity has not priced yet.
items.tweetB: SpaceX bond wobble as leading indicator: when the most marquee AI-adjacent credit deal shows fast money looking for exits on first-day trading, you are past the phase where everyone wants to own the story. You are in the phase where everyone wants to have owned it. That is a different trade entirely.
